rationale: The citing paper uses Jhang, Grant, and Campbell (2012) repeatedly to build its theory that extreme incongruity hampers benefit comprehension and adoption, and to motivate incongruity resolution as a key mechanism. It explicitly positions its contribution against that work in the discussion, noting that prior research focused on cognitive-flexibility facilitation for incongruous features, whereas this paper tests innovation locus (core vs peripheral) and RNI versus INI adoption. The citation is also operationally important because the authors adapt Jhang et al.'s benefit-understanding items in Study 4.
relation_description: The citing paper extends Jhang et al. (2012) by transferring the extreme incongruity-resolution logic from feature-level manipulations to a product design strategy (innovation locus) and linking it to adoption outcomes. It adds new mediating mechanisms and conditions (risk and usage flexibility; RNI versus INI) while retaining the prior insight that resolving incongruity improves evaluation.
